text: Evolving concepts for a modern minimally invasive management of perforated acute diverticulitis.
Diverticular disease is a common condition and perforated diverticulitis is associated with a significantly morbid surgical treatment.
Traditionally based on a midline laparotomy and Hartman sigmoid resection, with end colostomy.
Recently, laparoscopic lavage emerged as an effective minimally invasive alternative for patients with perforated diverticulitis and purulent peritonitis.
A randomized controlled trial is ongoing to definitely give evidence of safety and efficacy of this minimally invasive treatment.
The surgeon induces pneumoperitoneum using the open Hasson technique and inserts three or four trocars, similar to the setup for a left laparoscopic colectomy.
The grade of peritonitis and hinchy stage should be carefully assessed in order to exclude fecal peritonitis.
The surgeon identifies a probable perforation site and attempts laparoscopic suture to close the defect.
The surgeon performs copious lavage of all abdominal quadrants, followed by the insertion of one or two abdominal drains.
Open surgery and end colostomy increases length of stay and cost and negatively affects quality of life.
Laparoscopic treatment by non-resectional lavage and drainage has potential of improving health and reducing cost.

After resolution of the acute phase with laplavage, antibiotics, and avoidance of the colostomy, and excluding malignancy with follow-up colonoscopy, the elective resection is planned four to six months later.
The surgeon performs a single incision laparoscopic sigmoid resection, utilizing minimally invasive techniques to access and resect the sigmoid colon.
Through a three-centimeter transumbilical skin incision, the surgeon inserts a multiport device. The sigmoid colon is resected using a harmonic scalpel, with visible tissue cutting and separation.
The surgeon performs laparoscopic adhesiolysis through a single incision, using either sharp dissection with cold scissors or a harmonic scalpel, or gentle blunt dissection to separate adhesions.
In this case, a sixty-eight-year-old female patient has been treated initially with a lap lavage for Hinchi III perforated diverticulitis with purulent peritonitis.
After resolution of the acute phase and avoidance of the colostomy, malignancy was excluded with follow-up colonoscopy and elective resection was planned for six months later.
The surgeon performs a single incision laparoscopic sigmoidectomy with intracorporeal anastomosis. The resected specimen is extracted through the umbilical incision, leaving a three-centimeter scar.

open surgery and end colostomy increases length of stay and cost and negatively affects quality of life laparoscopic treatment by non resectional lavage and drainage has potential of improving health and reducing cost

Single incision laparoscopic sigmoidectomy is an attractive alternative to either open colectomy or traditional multi-part laparoscopy for elective management of diverticular disease. Advantages include better cosmetic results and less pain, even when compared with traditional laparoscopy where multiple ports are used.
And an additional incision, usually fenestal, for specimen extraction is needed.
Cells sigmoid resection is also feasible with low cost using a surgical glove port technique.
The surgeon performs a sigmoid resection using a 5mm laparoscopic camera and a flexible endo stapler to complete the distal resection of the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon performs a wide mobilization of the descending colon from the left paracolic gutter using a single incision laparoscopic approach.
The surgeon positions the distal stapled edge of the colon to reach the umbilicus, facilitating its extraction through a wound protector, followed by proximal resection.
The specimen is extracted through the umbilical incision using a wound protector. The proximal section is completed with the insertion of an anvil.
Following the reinduction of pneumoperitoneum and mobilization of the descending colon, the surgeon performs an intracorporeal anastomosis using a circular stapler inserted transanally.
The surgeon verifies the correct orientation of the proximal colon, ensuring that the mesentery is not twisted.

Accepted and feasible surgical approach for a case of perforated diverticulitis, HINCI-III, with a diffuse purulent peritonitis, can either be Hoffman's sigmoid resection, or laparoscopic lavage and drainage, or open or laparoscopic sigmoid resection and primary colorectal anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a hydronomatic test to check for leaks in the anastomosis, confirming the integrity of the surgical connection. No abdominal drains are left in place following the procedure.
The post-operative measurement is according to enhanced recovery program guidelines.

Combination of lap lavage and SILS resection respectively in urgent and elective settings are an innovative and promising minimal invasive management for patients with perforated acute diverticulitis.
